content.title: Funds Release Dogfood 2026-06-05
content.description: Controls the release of funds held in a blocked or pending state, once all verification, approval and compliance conditions have been satisfied.
content.trigger: A drawdown request from the front office for an approved facility; a held payment reaching its scheduled release date; or an Operations Analyst manually initiating release of a flagged item.
content.frequency: Continuous during business hours, per-request, no batch window. Indicative volume: 120–150 release items per day, spiking at month-end.
content.scopeIn: Release against an approved corporate credit facility; manual and straight-through (STP) release paths.
content.scopeOut: Origination or approval of the underlying credit facility; retail customer payments; cross-border settlement mechanics; trade-finance proceeds release; cash-collateral release (each handled by dedicated flows).
content.processInput: Facility ID, amount, currency, value date, beneficiary details, supporting documents.
content.processOutput: Posted fund movement, confirmation to front office, closed workflow item with complete audit log.

What happens.evidence: The release request arrives in the Operations queue via the Payments Workflow Tool. Each item carries a facility ID, amount, currency, value date and beneficiary details.

Inputs.evidence: Trigger: a drawdown request is received from the front office for an approved facility; a held payment reaches its scheduled release date; or an Operations Analyst manually initiates release of a flagged item. Each item carries a facility ID, amount, currency, value date and beneficiary details.

Outputs.evidence: no unique case reference is generated at intake today, the item is just deposited in the queue

Why it matters.evidence: items still arrive by email or phone and get keyed in manually — the Payments Workflow Tool is the mandated intake channel precisely to give every item a traceable audit record from entry

content.title: Receive Request
content.description: The release request arrives in the Operations queue via the Payments Workflow Tool. Each item carries a facility ID, amount, currency, value date and beneficiary details. The trigger may be a drawdown request from the front office, a held payment reaching its scheduled release date, or an Operations Analyst manually initiating release of a flagged item.
inputs: Drawdown request or scheduled release trigger from Front Office
Facility ID
Requested amount and currency
Value date
Beneficiary details
outputs: Queued release item in Payments Workflow Tool
Item metadata (facility ID, amount, currency, value date, beneficiary) available for validation
content.businessValue: This step establishes the formal entry point for the release process. Without a structured intake via the Payments Workflow Tool, subsequent validation and approval steps cannot execute against a consistent, traceable record.

transitions.when: Release request arrived in queue

What happens.evidence: The analyst stops at the first failed check and returns it immediately rather than running all four. There is effectively a fifth check: the value date is validated against the bank-holiday calendar, and the system enforces that, not the analyst. A returned item stays in the system with a 'returned' status so the front office resubmits against the same item.

Inputs.evidence: Queued release item from Payments Workflow Tool. The Operations Analyst checks completeness: the facility ID exists and is in "Active" status, the requested amount does not exceed the available undrawn limit, and the supporting documents are attached.

Outputs.evidence: a returned item stays in the system with a 'returned' status so the analyst can track it and the front office resubmits against the same item — it doesn't vanish

Why it matters.evidence: Incomplete requests are returned to the front office — this is the most common reason for delay.

content.title: Validate Request
content.description: The Operations Analyst checks the release request against four completeness criteria, stopping at the first failure and returning the item immediately: (1) the facility ID exists and is in Active status; (2) the requested amount does not exceed the available undrawn limit; (3) the value date is a valid business day — enforced by the system against the bank-holiday calendar; (4) supporting documents are attached. Incomplete requests are returned to the front office; the item stays in the system with a 'returned' status so the analyst can track it and the front office can resubmit against the same item record.
inputs: Queued release item from Payments Workflow Tool
Facility status from Facility Management System
Available undrawn limit data
Supporting documents attached to the request
outputs: Validated release item cleared for compliance screening
Returned item (status: 'returned') — stays in the system for front-office resubmission against the same item record
content.businessValue: Validation prevents incomplete or out-of-limit requests from progressing to approval and execution. It is the primary gate against facility breaches and is the most common source of process delays when the front office submits insufficient data.

transitions.when: Request is complete and valid
Request is incomplete — returned to Front Office for correction
Request is incomplete or invalid — returned to Front Office, exception raised

What happens.evidence: The item is screened for sanctions and AML hits. Clean items pass automatically; potential hits are routed to Compliance for adjudication. A confirmed hit freezes the item pending investigation.

Inputs.evidence: Y — accept (SME confirmed the inputs list in the foundational-run session)

Outputs.evidence: Clean items pass automatically; potential hits are routed to Compliance for adjudication. A confirmed hit freezes the item pending investigation.

Why it matters.evidence: Y — accept (SME confirmed the Why it matters block in the foundational-run session)

content.title: Compliance Screening
content.description: The item is screened for sanctions and AML hits using the Sanctions Screening Engine. Clean items pass automatically and proceed to first-line approval. Potential hits are routed to Compliance for adjudication. A confirmed hit freezes the item pending investigation.
inputs: Validated release item from Validate Request step
Beneficiary data
Sanctions and AML screening data in the Screening Engine
outputs: Clean screening result — item proceeds to First-Line Approval
Potential-hit alert — item routed to Compliance queue
Confirmed-hit freeze — item placed on hold pending investigation
content.businessValue: Compliance screening is the primary control against releasing funds to sanctioned or high-risk parties. Automating the clean-pass path maintains STP throughput while ensuring every item has been screened before any human approval is given.

transitions.when: Item screens clean — no sanctions or AML hits
Potential hit detected — item routed to Compliance for adjudication
Compliance adjudicates and clears a potential hit — item returns to normal flow at first-line approval
Confirmed sanctions or AML hit — item frozen, MLRO/SAR path initiated

What happens.evidence: The Operations Analyst approves the release after confirming validation and screening results.

Inputs.evidence: Confirming validation and screening results. Each item carries a facility ID, amount, currency, value date and beneficiary details.

Outputs.evidence: Y — accept (SME confirmed the outputs block; item is forwarded to Liquidity Confirmation or Second-Line Approval)

Why it matters.evidence: The analyst sees the raw detail, not just a green tick: the facility-check figures and the screening output are both visible, so the human-checkpoint framing holds

content.title: First-Line Approval
content.description: The Operations Analyst reviews the validated and screened release item and approves it for execution after confirming that the validation and screening results are satisfactory.
inputs: Validation result from Validate Request step
Compliance screening result from Compliance Screening step
Release item details (facility ID, amount, currency, value date, beneficiary)
outputs: First-line approval recorded in Payments Workflow Tool
Item forwarded to Liquidity Confirmation (if above Treasury threshold) or directly to Second-Line Approval
content.businessValue: First-line approval provides a documented human checkpoint before funds are committed. It ensures the analyst has personally reviewed validation and screening outcomes, establishing the first half of the mandatory 4-eyes segregation of duties control.
content.condition: Item has passed validation and compliance screening

transitions.when: Amount is at or above EUR 5,000,000 equivalent
Amount is below Treasury threshold — proceeds directly to second-line approval
Analyst spots a problem that validation or screening missed — item returned to Validate Request or back to the front office

What happens.evidence: For amounts at or above the Treasury threshold (currently EUR 5,000,000 equivalent), Treasury confirms funding availability for the value date. If funding is unavailable the release is deferred and rescheduled to the next available value date.

Inputs.evidence: Treasury threshold currently EUR 5,000,000 equivalent. Treasury confirms funding availability for the value date.

Outputs.evidence: Y — accept (SME confirmed: confirmed items proceed to Second-Line Approval; deferred items park and re-enter at Second-Line Approval on the revised date)

Why it matters.evidence: Y — accept (SME confirmed the framing — confirms the bank is not committing to a release it cannot fund)

content.title: Liquidity Confirmation
content.description: For release amounts at or above the Treasury threshold (currently EUR 5,000,000 equivalent; owned by Treasury/ALCO and reviewed quarterly), Treasury confirms that funding is available for the requested value date using the Treasury / Liquidity Platform. Confirmation is on-demand up to the hard internal cut-off of approximately 14:00 CET, after which Treasury will not confirm for same-day value regardless of queue. If funding is unavailable for the requested date, the item is parked in a deferred/awaiting-funding sub-state; it re-enters the process at Second-Line Approval once Treasury confirms the revised value date.
inputs: First-line approved release item with value date and amount
Funding availability data from Treasury / Liquidity Platform
Treasury threshold configuration (currently EUR 5,000,000 equivalent)
outputs: Treasury funding confirmation — item proceeds to Second-Line Approval
Deferral notice — item rescheduled with revised value date
content.businessValue: Liquidity confirmation ensures the bank is not committing to a release it cannot fund on the value date, particularly for large exposures above the EUR 5,000,000 threshold.
content.condition: Release amount is at or above the Treasury threshold (currently EUR 5,000,000 equivalent)

transitions.when: Treasury confirms funding availability for the requested value date
Funding unavailable on requested date — item parked in deferred/awaiting-funding state; re-enters at Second-Line Approval once Treasury confirms the revised value date
Insufficient funding for value date — item deferred pending Treasury confirmation of revised date

What happens.evidence: A separate Operations Approver independently reviews and authorises the release. The approver must not be the same person who performed the first-line approval.

Inputs.evidence: Y — accept (SME confirmed the inputs block)

Outputs.evidence: Y — accept (SME confirmed the outputs block)

Why it matters.evidence: Segregation of duties (4-eyes): First-line and second-line approval must be performed by different people. Preventive / manual.

content.title: Second-Line Approval (4-Eyes)
content.description: A separate Operations Approver confirms the approval chain, verifies identity segregation, reviews the screening and Treasury outcomes, and reviews the item summary before authorising the release. The Payments Workflow Tool technically blocks the same user ID from performing both approvals, backed by the C-3 segregation-of-duties policy; the approver does not repeat the full validation or screening checks.
inputs: First-line approved (and, where applicable, Treasury-confirmed) release item
Identity of the first-line approver (to verify segregation)
outputs: Second-line authorisation recorded in Payments Workflow Tool
Item cleared for execution in Core Banking System
content.businessValue: The 4-eyes control is a mandatory preventive safeguard that reduces the risk of unauthorised or erroneous fund releases. It satisfies the segregation of duties requirement and provides an independent review before irreversible account postings are made.
content.condition: Item has first-line approval; approver must be a different individual from the first-line approver

transitions.when: Second-line approver independently authorises the release
Second-line approver rejects — item returned to first-line analyst for rework, or cancelled outright if the release should not proceed
Four-eyes approver unavailable or SoD breach — item deferred or escalated to Operations Team Lead

What happens.evidence: The approved item is posted in the Core Banking System. Funds move from the held account to the beneficiary instruction.

Inputs.evidence: The approved item is posted. Each item carries a facility ID, amount, currency, value date and beneficiary details.

Outputs.evidence: Y — accept (SME confirmed: posted debit/credit in CBS, execution record in audit log)

Why it matters.evidence: Y — accept (SME confirmed the framing: irreversible financial action, only after both approval gates)

content.title: Execute Release
content.description: The approved release item is posted in the Core Banking System. Funds move from the held account to the beneficiary instruction, completing the financial movement.
inputs: Fully approved release item (first-line and second-line approvals confirmed)
Beneficiary account and payment instruction details
Value date confirmed as valid business day
outputs: Posted debit to held account and credit to beneficiary instruction in Core Banking System
Execution record in audit log including actor and timestamp
content.businessValue: Execution is the irreversible financial action — once posted, the funds move. Ensuring this step occurs only after both approval gates protects the bank against unauthorised releases and provides a traceable record for reconciliation and audit.

transitions.when: Posting completed successfully in Core Banking System
Core Banking System rejects the posting (technical error, duplicate, settlement reject) — item routed to analyst for manual investigation and controlled retry before re-attempting execution
Same-day cut-off missed — item re-queued for next-business-day execution

What happens.evidence: A confirmation is sent to the front office and the workflow item is closed. The audit log records all actors and timestamps.

Inputs.evidence: Y — accept (SME confirmed the inputs block)

Outputs.evidence: A confirmation is sent to the front office and the workflow item is closed. The audit log records all actors and timestamps.

Why it matters.evidence: Y — accept (SME confirmed: confirmation completes the lifecycle, informs Front Office, and seals the audit record)

content.title: Confirm and Close
content.description: The Payments Workflow Tool sends an automated notification to the Front Office confirming that the release has been executed, and the workflow item status flips to closed. The audit log records all actors and timestamps for the full lifecycle of the release; entries are write-once and append-only — they cannot be amended after the fact. If the automated notification fails, there is no automatic retry; the analyst follows up manually.
inputs: Execution confirmation from Core Banking System
Workflow item in Payments Workflow Tool (open, awaiting closure)
outputs: Confirmation notification sent to Front Office
Workflow item closed in Payments Workflow Tool
Complete audit log entry with all actors and timestamps
content.businessValue: Confirmation and closure completes the process lifecycle, ensures the Front Office is informed of the outcome, and seals the audit record.

Responsibility.evidence: Operations Analyst — intake, validation, first-line approval, execution and confirmation.

In this process.evidence: Update the In this process block so the closing confirmation reads as an automated Payments Workflow Tool notification, with the analyst only following up manually if it fails

content.title: Operations Analyst
content.responsibility: Owns intake, validation, first-line approval, execution and confirmation of fund release requests.
content.inThisProcess: The Operations Analyst receives the release request in the Payments Workflow Tool, validates completeness and facility status, confirms screening results, provides first-line approval, posts the approved item in the Core Banking System (or handles manual investigation and retry if the posting fails), and closes the workflow item. The Payments Workflow Tool sends the closing confirmation to the front office automatically; the analyst follows up manually only if the automated notification fails.

In this process.evidence: Reflect the narrower review scope (approval chain, identity segregation, screening/Treasury outcomes, item summary — not a full re-validation) and the reject-and-return authority

content.title: Operations Approver
content.responsibility: Provides independent second-line authorisation as the 4-eyes check before funds are released.
content.inThisProcess: The Operations Approver confirms the approval chain, verifies identity segregation, reviews the screening and Treasury outcomes, and reviews the item summary at the second-line approval step. They do not repeat the full validation or screening checks. The Payments Workflow Tool enforces identity segregation by blocking the same user ID from performing both approvals. The approver can reject and return the item to the first-line analyst for rework, or cancel it outright if the release should not proceed.

Responsibility.evidence: Treasury — funding availability confirmation above the threshold.

In this process.evidence: Pull in the threshold ownership (Treasury/ALCO, quarterly review), the hard ~14:00 CET same-day cut-off, and the deferred/awaiting-funding parking that re-enters at second-line approval

content.title: Treasury
content.responsibility: Confirms funding availability for large-value releases above the Treasury threshold before execution.
content.inThisProcess: Treasury is engaged at the Liquidity Confirmation step for release amounts at or above the EUR 5,000,000 threshold (owned by Treasury/ALCO and reviewed quarterly). Treasury confirms whether funding is available for the requested value date via the Treasury / Liquidity Platform; confirmation is on-demand up to the hard internal cut-off of approximately 14:00 CET, after which Treasury will not confirm for same-day value regardless of the queue. If funding is unavailable, the item is parked in a deferred/awaiting-funding state and re-enters the process at Second-Line Approval once Treasury confirms the revised value date.

Responsibility.evidence: Compliance — sanctions and AML adjudication. [...] potential hits are routed to Compliance for adjudication.

In this process.evidence: a Compliance analyst actively watches the screening queue and triages every alert — they're not passive

content.title: Compliance
content.responsibility: Performs sanctions and AML adjudication for items that generate potential hits during automated screening.
content.inThisProcess: A Compliance analyst actively watches the screening queue and triages every alert generated by the Sanctions Screening Engine during the Compliance Screening step. Compliance adjudicates each potential hit; a confirmed hit freezes the item pending investigation, while cleared items are returned to the normal release flow at first-line approval.

Responsibility.evidence: Front Office — submits drawdown requests; receives confirmation. [...] release against an approved corporate credit facility.

In this process.evidence: a chunk of items still arrive by email or phone from the front office and get keyed in manually

content.title: Front Office
content.responsibility: Submits drawdown requests against approved credit facilities and receives release confirmations.
content.inThisProcess: Front Office initiates the process by submitting a drawdown request carrying facility ID, amount, currency, value date and beneficiary details. A portion of requests still arrive by email or phone and are manually keyed into the queue by the Operations Analyst, rather than all coming through the clean digital path. Incomplete submissions are returned for correction, making request quality the primary driver of processing delays. Front Office receives the closing confirmation — an automated notification from the Payments Workflow Tool — at the end of the process.

Responsibility.evidence: Operations Team Lead — reassignment and escalation. [...] C-5 — Daily reconciliation of held vs. released balances. Detective / manual. Control owner: Operations Team Lead.

In this process.evidence: escalation has a defined trigger: items approaching or past the same-day cut-off still unresolved, or items breaching the SLA target. The Team Lead does actively monitor the queue and balance workload across analysts during the day, especially at month-end peaks

content.title: Operations Team Lead
content.responsibility: Owns reassignment, escalation and the daily reconciliation of held versus released balances.
content.inThisProcess: The Operations Team Lead actively monitors the queue and balances workload across analysts throughout the day, with particular intensity at month-end peaks. Escalation is triggered by defined conditions — items approaching or past the same-day cut-off still unresolved, or items breaching the SLA target — making it a documented control point rather than a purely informal safety net, with judgement applied on top. The Team Lead also handles reassignment of stalled or exceptional items and owns the detective control of daily reconciliation between held and released balances (C-5).

The FMS staleness qualifies this control's reliability: if the limit data is a few minutes old, an over-limit request could slip through

Risk addressed.evidence: C-1 — Facility limit check. Release is blocked unless the available facility limit covers the requested amount. Preventive / automated.

content.title: Facility Limit Check
content.whatItChecks: Verifies that the requested release amount does not exceed the available undrawn limit on the associated facility, and that the facility is in active status.
content.controlActivity: The Payments Workflow Tool queries the Facility Management System for the facility's current status and available undrawn limit, then applies the block if the limit is insufficient or the facility is inactive. The FMS supplies the data; the PWT owns the enforcement mechanism. The analyst reviews the returned figures as part of the validation step. Known limitation: the FMS can serve data a few minutes stale (see PP-FRDB-004), meaning the block may not reflect the real-time limit position — an over-limit request could slip through if the stale figure shows sufficient headroom.
content.riskAddressed: Prevents over-drawing an approved facility and releasing funds against an inactive or non-existent credit line.
content.timing: Runs at the point of request validation (step 2) for every release request, before any approval is sought.

What it checks.evidence: The item is screened for sanctions and AML hits. Clean items pass automatically; potential hits are routed to Compliance for adjudication.

Control activity.evidence: frozen item follows a defined Compliance investigation procedure with an internal SLA (~1 business day to triage), escalates to MLRO / Financial Crime team for release, rejection or SAR. High false-positive rate reduces throughput and creates adjudication backlog at peak volume

Risk addressed.evidence: The process is subject to anti-money-laundering and sanctions obligations (EU sanctions regulations, AML directives).

content.title: Sanctions and AML Screening
content.whatItChecks: Screens every release item against sanctions lists and AML indicators before funds are released, flagging any potential hits for adjudication.
content.controlActivity: The Sanctions Screening Engine runs automatically on each item. Clean items pass automatically. Potential hits are routed to the Compliance function for manual adjudication. A confirmed hit freezes the item; the Compliance analyst follows a defined investigation procedure with an internal SLA of approximately 1 business day to triage. Items that remain frozen after triage escalate to the MLRO / Financial Crime team, who determine the outcome: release, rejection, or filing of a suspicious-activity report. Known limitation: the hit rate routed to Compliance is high relative to true hits, creating a recurring adjudication backlog at peak volume that delays clean-path releases (see PP-FRDB-005).
content.riskAddressed: Prevents the bank from releasing funds to sanctioned parties or facilitating money laundering.
content.timing: Executed during the compliance screening step (step 3), before first-line approval, on every item processed.

What it checks.evidence: C-3 — Segregation of duties (4-eyes). First-line and second-line approval must be performed by different people.

Control activity.evidence: documented access-management process where first-line and second-line entitlements are provisioned separately and subject to periodic access recertification. Small approver pool near cut-off creates bottleneck — operating effectiveness limitation (PP-FRDB-008)

Risk addressed.evidence: C-3 — Segregation of duties (4-eyes). First-line and second-line approval must be performed by different people. Preventive / manual.

Timing.evidence: First-line approval (step 4). Second-line approval (4-eyes) (step 6). The approver must not be the same person who performed the first-line approval.

content.title: Segregation of Duties (4-Eyes)
content.whatItChecks: Ensures that the person who performs first-line approval is not the same person who performs second-line authorisation, enforcing dual-person integrity on every release.
content.controlActivity: The Payments Workflow Tool technically blocks the same user ID from performing both first-line and second-line approval on a single item, enforcing the segregation rule at the system level. The second-line approver's review of the approval chain and item summary is a separate manual step. First-line and second-line entitlements are provisioned separately under a documented access-management process and are subject to periodic access recertification; this recertification is what prevents one person from holding both roles, and the control's strength depends on it running. Known operating-effectiveness limitation: the authorised approver pool is small — when it is thin near the cut-off, items bottleneck waiting for a second pair of eyes (see PP-FRDB-008).
content.riskAddressed: Guards against unauthorised release by ensuring no single individual can approve a payment unilaterally.
content.timing: Applied at both the first-line approval step (step 4) and the second-line approval step (step 6) for every release item.

What it checks.evidence: threshold owned by Treasury/ALCO and reviewed quarterly; FX conversion at time of check determines in-scope status for non-EUR amounts

Control activity.evidence: hard ~14:00 CET same-day cut-off after which Treasury will not confirm; deferred items park in deferred/awaiting-funding state and re-enter at second-line approval once revised date confirmed

Risk addressed.evidence: FX-threshold ambiguity: near-threshold non-EUR amounts can flip above or below the EUR 5m line on a small rate move (PP-FRDB-007)

Timing.evidence: must complete before the ~14:00 CET hard same-day cut-off; items confirmed after cut-off cannot achieve same-day value

content.title: Treasury Funding Confirmation
content.whatItChecks: Confirms that sufficient funding is available for the requested value date before releasing amounts at or above the Treasury threshold (currently EUR 5,000,000 equivalent; owned by Treasury/ALCO and reviewed quarterly). For non-EUR amounts, the FX conversion at time of check determines whether the item falls in or out of scope — near-threshold amounts whose converted EUR equivalent sits close to the line can flip above or below it on a small rate move.
content.controlActivity: Treasury manually confirms funding availability for the value date via the Treasury / Liquidity Platform. Confirmation is on-demand for in-scope items up to the hard internal same-day cut-off of approximately 14:00 CET, after which Treasury will not confirm for same-day value regardless of the queue. If funding is available, the item proceeds to second-line approval. If funding is unavailable, the item is parked in a deferred/awaiting-funding state and re-enters the process at second-line approval once Treasury confirms a revised value date. C-4 is classified as preventive and manual.
content.riskAddressed: Prevents the bank from committing to a large-value release on a value date for which funding is insufficient. Also addresses the FX-threshold ambiguity (see PP-FRDB-007): near-threshold non-EUR items require FX conversion to determine whether the control applies, and a small rate move can change that determination; items whose in-scope status is ambiguous due to FX movement should be treated as in-scope pending Treasury confirmation.
content.timing: Triggered for every item at or above the Treasury threshold during Liquidity Confirmation (PS-FRDB-005), before second-line approval. Must complete before the ~14:00 CET hard same-day cut-off; items confirmed after cut-off cannot achieve same-day value.

What it checks.evidence: CBS is the system of record for held-account balances and postings; PWT is the system of record for authorised/released items

Control activity.evidence: end of business after CBS settlement run; short look-back of a few days. Escalation: Head of Payment Operations → Compliance/Financial Crime if unauthorised/unmatched; Finance where there's a GL impact

Risk addressed.evidence: Y — accept (SME confirmed: detects discrepancies including unmatched releases, posting errors and potential unauthorised movements)

Timing.evidence: runs at end of business after the CBS settlement run completes, covering that day's released items with a short look-back (a few days) to catch late or amended settlements

content.title: Daily Reconciliation of Held vs. Released Balances
content.whatItChecks: Reconciles held-account balances and postings recorded in the Core Banking System against the released items authorised and recorded in the Payments Workflow Tool. The CBS is the system of record for balances; the PWT is the system of record for what was authorised and released.
content.controlActivity: The Operations Team Lead performs the reconciliation manually at end of business after the CBS settlement run completes, covering that day's released items with a short look-back of a few days to catch late or amended settlements. On finding a discrepancy, the Team Lead escalates first to the Head of Payment Operations. If the discrepancy could indicate an unauthorised or unmatched release, it is escalated further to Compliance / Financial Crime; Finance is informed where there is a general-ledger impact. C-5 is classified as detective and manual.
content.riskAddressed: Detects discrepancies between held and released balances not caught by preventive controls during the day, including unmatched releases, posting errors and potential unauthorised movements.
content.timing: Performed once daily at end of business, after the Core Banking System settlement run completes, covering same-day released items with a short look-back of a few days for late or amended settlements.

Control activity.evidence: the Payments Workflow Tool blocks any posting unless both approval flags (first- and second-line) are present — a posting cannot be initiated without the complete approval chain

Risk addressed.evidence: an irreversible step with no documented control is an audit red flag — the control prevents execution of an incompletely approved release

content.title: Dual-Approval Posting Gate
content.whatItChecks: Verifies that both first-line and second-line approval flags are present in the Payments Workflow Tool before a posting can be triggered in the Core Banking System.
content.controlActivity: The Payments Workflow Tool blocks the execute-release action unless both approval flags are set. A posting cannot be initiated without the complete approval chain; the system enforces this automatically for every item.
content.riskAddressed: Prevents execution of a release that has not completed the mandatory 4-eyes approval chain, guarding against unauthorised or incompletely approved fund movements.
content.timing: Applied at the point of execution (Execute Release step) for every release item, before the Core Banking System posting is triggered.

content.title: Intake Completeness Control
content.whatItChecks: Confirms that a drawdown request received at PS-FRDB-001 carries all mandatory fields required to proceed to validation — facility ID, requested amount and currency, value date, beneficiary account, and submission channel reference.
content.controlActivity: The Payments Workflow Tool enforces mandatory-field completion before a submission is accepted. For digital portal submissions, the system blocks incomplete submissions at the point of entry. For email and phone submissions, the Operations Analyst applies a structured intake checklist before creating the PWT case record.
content.riskAddressed: Prevents items with incomplete or miskeyed data from reaching the validation queue, reducing downstream return-to-sender cycles and ensuring the audit trail is complete from the point of intake.
content.timing: Applied at PS-FRDB-001 for every submission before the case record is created and the item enters the operations queue.

Why it applies.evidence: Y — accept: outbound payment from corporate credit facility could constitute sanctions violation or money-laundering conduit; EU FTR payer/payee requirements also apply

How it is met.evidence: expand to full picture: C-2 screening, Compliance active queue triage, MLRO/SAR escalation path, write-once audit log at PS-FRDB-008

content.title: AML and Sanctions Obligations
content.whatItRequires: The EU Anti-Money-Laundering Directives (notably 6AMLD) and applicable EU Council restrictive-measures (sanctions) regulations require that every payment be screened against applicable sanctions lists and assessed for money-laundering risk before funds are released. The bank is also required to report suspicious transactions to the relevant Financial Intelligence Unit (FIU) via the MLRO. Payer/payee information requirements under the EU Funds Transfer Regulation (2015/847) are covered in REG-FRDB-002.
content.whyItApplies: The process releases funds held against corporate credit facilities; any such outbound payment could constitute a sanctions violation or money-laundering conduit if not screened prior to execution. The involvement of identified beneficiaries and fund movements across accounts also brings the process within the scope of the EU Funds Transfer Regulation's payer/payee information requirements.
content.howItIsMet: CP-FRDB-002 screens every item against sanctions lists before release. Confirmed hits escalate to the MLRO for SAR determination. The write-once audit log at PS-FRDB-008 provides the regulatory record trail for all actors and timestamps.

What it requires.evidence: DORA EU 2022/2554 applies — ICT-dependent payment process; also EBA ICT/operational-risk guidelines. REG-FRDB-002 is the dedicated FTR home; FTR text trimmed from REG-FRDB-001 to a cross-reference

Why it applies.evidence: ICT-dependent payment process on PWT and CBS — within DORA scope; fund movements to beneficiaries within FTR payer/payee scope

How it is met.evidence: expand to: step-2 validation (FTR), CP-FRDB-006 dual-approval gate, PS-FRDB-008 write-once audit log, CP-FRDB-005 CBS/PWT reconciliation

content.title: EU Funds Transfer Regulation and Operational Resilience
content.whatItRequires: The EU Funds Transfer Regulation (EU 2015/847) requires that complete payer and payee information accompanies each payment executed by the bank. DORA (EU 2022/2554) and associated EBA ICT and operational-risk guidelines require that ICT-dependent processes such as payment execution are subject to adequate operational resilience controls, including risk management, incident response and continuity arrangements.
content.whyItApplies: The process executes fund movements to identified beneficiaries, bringing every release within scope of the FTR payer/payee information requirements. As an ICT-dependent payment process operating on the Payments Workflow Tool and Core Banking System, it is also within scope of DORA and EBA operational resilience expectations.
content.howItIsMet: CP-FRDB-001 confirms beneficiary details and limit completeness at PS-FRDB-002. CP-FRDB-006 enforces dual approval before posting. CP-FRDB-005 provides daily CBS reconciliation. PS-FRDB-008 write-once audit log meets FTR and DORA record-keeping requirements.

What it requires.evidence: https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2024/886/oj/eng — "PSPs shall carry out such verifications immediately after the entry into force of any new targeted financial restrictive measures...and at least once every calendar day" (Art. 5d(1)); "the payer's PSP and the payee's PSP...shall not verify whether the payer or the payee...are persons or entities subject to targeted financial restrictive measures in addition to carrying out verifications under paragraph 1" (Art. 5d(2)) — fetched 2026-06-06

Why it applies.evidence: https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2024/886/oj/eng — "the payee's PSP shall, within 10 seconds of the time of receipt of the payment order for an instant credit transfer by the payer's PSP, make the amount of the payment transaction available" (Art. 5a(4)(c)) — fetched 2026-06-06

How it is met.evidence: https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2024/886/oj/eng — "immediately upon receiving the confirmation of completion...the payer's PSP shall, free of charge, inform the payer...whether the amount of the payment transaction has been made available" (Art. 5a(4)(e)) — fetched 2026-06-06

content.title: EU Instant Payments Regulation (EU 2024/886)
content.whatItRequires: Requires eurozone PSPs to settle instant credit transfers within 10 seconds (Art. 5a(4)(c)); to conduct sanctions screening via daily list-check rather than per transaction (Art. 5d(1)–(2)); and to notify the payer immediately on completion. Eurozone sending deadline: 9 October 2025.
content.whyItApplies: The bank executes euro credit transfers for corporate drawdowns; as a eurozone PSP it falls within scope. The Regulation's daily-list screening model changes PS-FRDB-003 and its 10-second execution window constrains PS-FRDB-004 through PS-FRDB-008.
content.howItIsMet: Current As-Is uses per-transaction sanctions screening at PS-FRDB-003 — inconsistent with Art. 5d(2). Daily-list screening migration and immediate payer notification at PS-FRDB-008 required. Full compliance posture to be confirmed with the control-compliance specialist.

What it requires.evidence: https://www.eba.europa.eu/activities/single-rulebook/regulatory-activities/internal-governance/guidelines-internal-governance-under-crd — "enhance and consolidate supervisory expectations and ultimately to improve the sound implementation of internal governance arrangements" — application date 05 December 2021 confirmed — fetched 2026-06-06

Why it applies.evidence: https://www.eba.europa.eu/activities/single-rulebook/regulatory-activities/internal-governance/guidelines-internal-governance-under-crd — application date 05 December 2021 confirmed; guidelines cover governance arrangements for all EU credit institutions — fetched 2026-06-06

content.title: EBA Guidelines on Internal Governance (EBA/GL/2021/05)
content.source: EBA Guidelines on Internal Governance EBA/GL/2021/05
content.whatItRequires: EBA/GL/2021/05 (applicable 5 December 2021) requires EU credit institutions to implement sound governance including the four-eyes principle — no individual may both initiate and approve a transaction — and segregation of duties between initiation and authorisation roles.
content.whyItApplies: A Relationship Manager initiates the drawdown request; a Credit Operations analyst separately approves and posts it. The guidelines require this segregation as a formal governance obligation for all EU credit institutions, not merely an operational preference.
content.howItIsMet: CP-FRDB-003 (4-Eyes Control) and CP-FRDB-006 (Dual-Approval Gate) implement the four-eyes principle; ROLE-FRDB-001 through ROLE-FRDB-003 define the segregated roles. Full mapping to be confirmed with the control-compliance specialist.

What it requires.evidence: https://www.judict.eu/hla/32013R0575-X/article-395 — "An institution shall not incur an exposure, after taking into account the effect of the credit risk mitigation in accordance with Articles 399 to 403, to a client or a group of connected clients the value of which exceeds 25 % of its Tier 1 capital" (Art. 395(1)) — fetched 2026-06-06

Why it applies.evidence: https://www.judict.eu/hla/32013R0575-X/article-395 — Art. 395(1) 25% Tier 1 limit applies at point of exposure incurrence; each drawdown constitutes or increases a client exposure — fetched 2026-06-06

How it is met.evidence: https://www.eba.europa.eu/single-rule-book-qa/qna/view/publicId/2021_5837 — "the facility may be drawn only if it has been ascertained that it will not cause the limit applicable under Article 395(1) to be exceeded" — fetched 2026-06-06

content.title: CRR Large Exposures Limit (EU 575/2013, Art. 395)
content.whatItRequires: Article 395(1) CRR (EU 575/2013) prohibits institutions from incurring an exposure to a client or group of connected clients exceeding 25% of Tier 1 capital. A committed facility may be drawn only once it is ascertained the limit will not be breached.
content.whyItApplies: Each drawdown under a corporate credit facility creates or increases a balance-sheet exposure to the borrower. The bank must verify the 25% Tier 1 large exposure limit will not be breached before releasing funds.
content.howItIsMet: CP-FRDB-001 (Facility Limit Check) implements the pre-drawdown exposure check, querying SYS-FRDB-003 (Financial Management System). Whether the check explicitly covers the Art. 395 25% Tier 1 constraint (distinct from the internal facility limit) is to be confirmed.

What it requires.evidence: https://www.eba.europa.eu/regulation-and-policy/credit-risk/guidelines-on-loan-origination-and-monitoring — "robust and prudent standards for credit risk taking, management and monitoring" — application date 30 June 2021 confirmed — fetched 2026-06-06

Why it applies.evidence: https://www.eba.europa.eu/regulation-and-policy/credit-risk/guidelines-on-loan-origination-and-monitoring — application date 30 June 2021 confirmed; guidelines govern credit facility lifecycle including disbursement phase — fetched 2026-06-06

content.title: EBA Guidelines on Loan Origination and Monitoring (EBA/GL/2020/06)
content.source: EBA Guidelines on Loan Origination and Monitoring EBA/GL/2020/06
content.whatItRequires: EBA/GL/2020/06 (applicable 30 June 2021) requires institutions to establish documented policies for credit facility disbursement, covering pre-disbursement conditions verification, audit trail requirements throughout the disbursement lifecycle, and ongoing monitoring of the facility.
content.whyItApplies: The funds-release process is the disbursement phase of a corporate credit facility. The full lifecycle from request intake (PS-FRDB-001) through confirmation and audit-log closure (PS-FRDB-008) is within scope of these guidelines.
content.howItIsMet: PS-FRDB-001 through PS-FRDB-007 implement sequential pre-disbursement verification. CP-FRDB-001 through CP-FRDB-006 form the control framework. PS-FRDB-008 write-once audit log meets the audit trail requirement. Full coverage mapping to be confirmed with the control-compliance specialist.

Purpose.evidence: Payments Workflow Tool — queue, routing, approvals and audit log. — §Systems

Role in this process.evidence: identity-segregation block for C-3, FMS limit-check query enforcement for CP-FRDB-001, dual-approval posting gate CP-FRDB-006, automated FO notification with write-once audit log. Tier 1 criticality with tight RTO to be confirmed in IT-architecture pass

content.title: Payments Workflow Tool
content.purpose: Manages the end-to-end payment operations queue, routing, approvals workflow, and audit log. Business-critical orchestration system (Tier 1); RTO and RPO bands to be confirmed in the IT-architecture pass.
content.roleInThisProcess: The central orchestration platform for the release process. Receives incoming requests, routes items through validation, screening and approval steps, and enforces key controls: (1) blocks the same user ID from performing both first-line and second-line approval (C-3 identity-segregation); (2) queries the Facility Management System for limit data and applies the facility-limit block (CP-FRDB-001); (3) gates the Execute Release action unless both approval flags are present (CP-FRDB-006 dual-approval posting gate). On completion, sends the automated confirmation notification to the Front Office and maintains the write-once audit log recording all actors and timestamps across the full release lifecycle.

Purpose.evidence: Core Banking System — account postings and fund movement. — §Systems

Role in this process.evidence: occasional CBS latency at peak volume; rare failed postings needing manual investigation and retry (PP-FRDB-009). Tier 1 / system of record for balances; RTO to be confirmed in IT-architecture pass

content.title: Core Banking System
content.purpose: Holds account balances and executes postings that move funds between accounts. System of record for all balance positions. Business-critical (Tier 1); RTO and RPO bands to be confirmed in the IT-architecture pass.
content.roleInThisProcess: Triggered by the Payments Workflow Tool in step 7 (Execute Release) to post the approved item, moving funds from the held account to the beneficiary instruction. Known characteristic: occasional latency at peak volume and rare posting failures (technical errors, duplicates, settlement rejects) that require manual analyst investigation and a controlled retry before the release can be completed (see PP-FRDB-009).

Purpose.evidence: Sanctions Screening Engine — real-time sanctions / AML screening. — §Systems

Role in this process.evidence: Compliance queue with active analyst triage; MLRO/Financial Crime SAR path for confirmed hits (CP-FRDB-002); high false-positive rate known operating characteristic (PP-FRDB-005). Compliance-critical — screening outage must block releases. List-currency operational dependency: daily updates, stale lists are a screening risk

content.title: Sanctions Screening Engine
content.purpose: Performs real-time sanctions and AML screening of payment items. Compliance-critical: a screening outage must block releases, not bypass them. Operational dependency: sanctions and watchlists must be kept current from list providers (typically daily updates); stale lists are a material screening risk.
content.roleInThisProcess: Invoked by the Payments Workflow Tool during step 3 (Compliance Screening) to screen every release item. Clean items pass automatically and proceed to first-line approval. Potential hits are routed to the Compliance queue, where a Compliance analyst actively triages every alert. Confirmed hits escalate to the MLRO / Financial Crime team for determination of release, rejection or suspicious-activity report (see CP-FRDB-002). Known operating characteristic: the hit rate routed to Compliance is high relative to true hits, creating a recurring adjudication backlog at peak volume (see PP-FRDB-005).

Purpose.evidence: Treasury / Liquidity Platform — funding availability confirmation. — §Systems

Role in this process.evidence: ~14:00 CET hard cut-off; deferred/awaiting-funding mechanism; FX conversion for near-threshold non-EUR amounts. PWT ↔ Treasury/Liquidity Platform: Treasury uses platform directly, confirmation result flows back to PWT against the item. Critical-path for large-value same-day releases

content.title: Treasury / Liquidity Platform
content.purpose: Tracks funding availability and confirms Treasury's capacity to settle on a given value date. On the critical path for large-value same-day releases — an outage near the cut-off blocks same-day value for all in-scope items.
content.roleInThisProcess: Used by Treasury in step 5 (Liquidity Confirmation) to confirm funding availability for releases at or above the EUR 5,000,000 threshold (Treasury/ALCO, reviewed quarterly). For non-EUR amounts, the FX conversion at time of check determines whether the item crosses the threshold. Confirmation is on-demand up to the hard internal same-day cut-off of approximately 14:00 CET; after cut-off, Treasury will not confirm for same-day value. The confirmation result flows back to the Payments Workflow Tool against the item. If funding is unavailable, the item is parked in a deferred/awaiting-funding state in the PWT and re-enters at second-line approval once Treasury confirms a revised value date.

Purpose.evidence: Facility Management System — facility status and available limit. — §Systems

Role in this process.evidence: FMS can serve limit data a few minutes old (PP-FRDB-004), qualifying real-time reliability of CP-FRDB-001. PWT queries FMS for facility status and available limit. Data freshness is a monitored dependency

content.title: Facility Management System
content.purpose: Holds corporate credit facility records including current status and available undrawn limit. Supporting system, but data freshness is a monitored dependency: FMS unavailability or stale data degrades the facility-limit preventive control (CP-FRDB-001).
content.roleInThisProcess: Queried by the Payments Workflow Tool during step 2 (Validate Request) to confirm the facility is Active and the requested amount does not exceed the available undrawn limit (CP-FRDB-001). Known limitation: the FMS can serve limit data a few minutes stale, meaning the limit check may not reflect the real-time position — an over-limit request could slip through if the stale figure shows sufficient headroom (see PP-FRDB-004). Data freshness should be treated as a monitored operational dependency.

Definition.evidence: The target for a clean STP release is same business day, within 2 hours of receipt. — §Standard service level

Current reading.evidence: weekly ops report / PWT dashboard shows ~85–90% within target; not a governed KPI. Clock starts at PWT queue entry, not FO submission — email/phone manual-keying delay invisible to metric; SLA looks better than client-perceived turnaround

Why it matters.evidence: Y — accept: STP turnaround time is the headline service-level commitment for this process

content.title: STP Release Time
content.definition: End-to-end elapsed time from receipt of a clean STP release request to funds posted and front office confirmed, measured in hours.
content.currentReading: A weekly operations report and PWT dashboard track SLA compliance; approximately 85–90% of clean STP items currently complete within the 2-hour target. Measurement is not yet formalised as a governed KPI. Known measurement gap: the clock starts when the item enters the PWT queue, not at the front office's original submission — the manual-keying delay on email and phone items is invisible to this metric, meaning the stated compliance figure overstates client-perceived turnaround time.
content.whyItMatters: STP turnaround time is the headline service-level commitment for this process.
content.target: Same business day, within 2 hours of receipt
content.value: ~85–90% of clean STP items within target (weekly ops report / PWT dashboard; not yet a governed KPI)

Definition.evidence: Manual releases with no exception complete same business day. — §Standard service level

Current reading.evidence: manual releases folded into overall same-day figure, not broken out separately — measurement gap; same clock-start ambiguity as M-FRDB-001. SME recommends tracking separately as that's where SLA risk concentrates

Why it matters.evidence: Y — accept: manual release completion time is a stated SLA target; manual items are where SLA risk concentrates

content.title: Manual Release Time
content.definition: Elapsed time from receipt to completion for manual releases that require human intervention but do not involve an exception, measured in business days.
content.currentReading: Manual releases are not broken out separately in the weekly operations report; they are folded into the overall same-day completion figure, so there is no dedicated manual-release actual. Measurement gap: manual items — which carry the callback, exception-handling and return/rework paths — are the slowest items in the queue but the least visible in current reporting. The same clock-start ambiguity applies: the clock starts at PWT queue entry, not at original front-office submission. Recommended: track manual release time as a separate metric, as this is where same-day SLA risk concentrates.
content.whyItMatters: Manual release completion time is a stated service-level target for non-exception items.
content.value: Not tracked separately — folded into overall same-day completion figure

Definition.evidence: Exception resolution targets within 1 business day of identification. — §Standard service level

Current reading.evidence: Compliance tracks adjudication SLA (~1BD per CP-FRDB-002); incomplete requests and funding shortfalls not measured against resolution clock. No consolidated exception metric today. Recommend tracking by type: sanctions hit / incomplete request / funding shortfall

Why it matters.evidence: Y — accept: exception resolution time is a stated SLA target; different exception types have different owners and resolution paths

content.title: Exception Resolution Time
content.definition: Elapsed time from the moment an exception is identified — a sanctions hit, an incomplete request, a funding shortfall — to the moment it is resolved or formally escalated.
content.currentReading: Partially tracked: Compliance monitors its own adjudication SLA (approximately 1 business day, per CP-FRDB-002) for sanctions hits. Incomplete/returned requests and funding-shortfall deferrals are not measured against a resolution clock; there is no consolidated exception-resolution metric across types today. Measurement gap: exceptions are driven by different owners with different resolution paths — a single aggregated number obscures the variation. Recommended: track exception resolution time broken out by type (sanctions hit / incomplete request / funding shortfall), since their drivers, owners and SLA bands differ.
content.whyItMatters: Exception resolution time is a stated service-level target for this process.
content.value: Partially tracked — Compliance adjudication SLA (~1BD) monitored; incomplete requests and funding shortfalls not measured

Description.evidence: two patterns — only submission-quality defects (missing/incorrect facility ID, missing docs, bad value date) are a true pain point; over-limit cases are the control working as intended

Impact.evidence: Items frequently stall at validation when the front office submits an incomplete request or an inactive facility ID. — §Known pain points; Same-day value is at risk when a request arrives close to the 14:00 cut-off — §Known pain points

Root cause.evidence: no pre-validation gate on the front-office side before submission; high proportion of requests arrive by email/phone and bypass the structured form, so field completeness isn't enforced at source

content.title: Incomplete Front-Office Requests Stalling Validation
content.description: Release requests submitted by the front office frequently arrive with submission-quality defects — incorrect or inactive facility IDs, absent supporting documents, or invalid value dates — requiring the Operations Analyst to return the item before processing can continue. Over-limit amounts are a separate pattern: the facility-limit check (C-1) correctly blocking an over-limit request is the control working as intended, not a submission defect.
content.impact: Items stall at step 2 and must be returned to the front office, delaying same-day value and increasing the risk of breaching the 14:00 CET cut-off.
content.rootCause: No pre-submission validation gate exists on the front-office side before a request enters the Operations queue, so field completeness is not enforced at source. A high proportion of requests still arrive by email or phone and bypass the structured form entirely, removing even the basic mandatory-field enforcement the tool provides.

Description.evidence: keep scoped to Treasury-confirmation cut-off timing risk; cross-reference PP-FRDB-007, PP-FRDB-008 and month-end volume spikes as compounding contributors rather than absorbing them

Impact.evidence: Y — accept: missing cut-off defers settlement to next business day, breaching same-day SLA

Root cause.evidence: no early-warning or queue-prioritisation mechanism for items approaching cut-off; late-arriving requests accumulate and Treasury/second-line steps get squeezed into final window; nothing flags 'this item will miss same-day value unless actioned now'

content.title: Same-Day Value at Risk Near Cut-Off
content.description: Requests that arrive close to the 14:00 CET same-day-value cut-off and still require Treasury confirmation (PS-FRDB-005) risk missing same-day value if the Treasury or second-line approval steps are delayed. Compounding contributors are captured separately: Treasury analyst availability and FX threshold ambiguity near cut-off (PP-FRDB-007), the small authorised approver pool creating a bottleneck at second-line approval (PP-FRDB-008), and month-end volume spikes that push more items into the final window simultaneously.
content.impact: Missing the 14:00 CET cut-off defers settlement to the next business day, breaching the same-day service-level commitment and creating client-liquidity impact for corporate borrowers who needed same-day funds.
content.rootCause: No early-warning or queue-prioritisation mechanism exists for items approaching the cut-off. Late-arriving requests accumulate in the standard queue and the Treasury confirmation and second-line approval steps get compressed into the final window without any flag that an item will miss same-day value unless actioned immediately. The structural absence of cut-off-aware prioritisation is the core cause.

Description.evidence: a chunk of items still arrive by email or phone from the front office and get keyed in manually

Impact.evidence: we see queue pressure at month-end

content.title: Email and Phone Intake Requiring Manual Keying
content.description: A portion of release requests from the front office arrive by email or phone rather than through the Payments Workflow Tool, requiring Operations staff to manually key them into the queue before processing can begin.
content.impact: Adds manual transcription effort and transcription-error risk; contributes to queue pressure at month-end when volumes spike.
content.rootCause: Not elicited in this session — the SME noted the pattern but did not diagnose the underlying cause.

Description.evidence: the Facility Management System is sometimes slow to respond and the available-limit data can be a few minutes stale, which occasionally forces a re-check

Impact.evidence: occasionally forces a re-check — implies a re-check cycle adds elapsed time at step 2

content.title: FMS Stale Limit Data Forcing Re-checks at Validation
content.description: The Facility Management System sometimes responds slowly and the available-limit data it serves can be a few minutes stale, meaning the analyst's initial limit check may be based on an out-of-date figure and the check must be repeated once the data refreshes.
content.impact: Delays at the Validate Request step (PS-FRDB-002); adds elapsed time and creates uncertainty about whether the limit position has actually changed or is simply a data-refresh lag.
content.rootCause: Not elicited in this session — the SME noted the latency and staleness pattern but did not diagnose the underlying FMS architecture cause.

Description.evidence: the hit rate routed to Compliance is high relative to true hits, and there's a recurring adjudication backlog at peak volume that delays releases

Impact.evidence: a recurring adjudication backlog at peak volume that delays releases

content.title: High Screening False-Positive Rate Causing Adjudication Backlog
content.description: The Sanctions Screening Engine routes a high volume of potential hits to the Compliance adjudication queue relative to the number of true hits. During peak periods, this creates a recurring backlog in the Compliance queue that holds up release items awaiting a cleared decision.
content.impact: Releases are delayed while items queue for Compliance adjudication; the delay is most acute at peak volume (e.g. month-end), where the backlog can push items past the same-day-value cut-off.
content.rootCause: Not elicited in this session — the SME confirmed the pattern and backlog but did not diagnose whether the root cause is screening-rule calibration, insufficient Compliance capacity, or both.

Description.evidence: during peak periods approvals queue up, and there's a real risk of analysts rubber-stamping the green-tick items without opening the detail

Impact.evidence: real risk of analysts rubber-stamping the green-tick items without opening the detail — the human-checkpoint control loses its value if the detail is not reviewed

content.title: Peak-Period Approval Queue and Rubber-Stamping Risk at First-Line Approval
content.description: During peak periods, first-line approvals queue up faster than analysts can work through them. Under queue pressure there is a known risk of analysts approving items on the strength of the green-tick status summary without opening the underlying facility-check figures or screening output.
content.impact: If analysts skip the detail review, the first-line human checkpoint becomes a nominal sign-off rather than a substantive control, weakening the 4-eyes segregation-of-duties control (C-3) and increasing the risk that a problem missed by validation or screening passes through undetected.
content.rootCause: Not elicited in this session — the SME confirmed the pattern but did not diagnose whether the root cause is tool design (detail not surfaced prominently), volume, or time pressure.

Description.evidence: Treasury analyst availability right before cut-off, and FX conversion on near-threshold amounts that can flip an item above or below the EUR 5m line

Impact.evidence: can cause same-day-value failures; FX flip changes whether confirmation is required, creating uncertainty

content.title: Treasury Availability and FX Threshold Ambiguity
content.description: Two friction points at Liquidity Confirmation: Treasury analyst availability is constrained before the 14:00 CET cut-off; near-threshold non-EUR amounts require FX conversion whose result can flip an item above or below the EUR 5,000,000 threshold.
content.impact: Constrained Treasury availability near cut-off risks same-day-value failures. FX threshold flips create uncertainty about whether confirmation is required.
content.rootCause: Not elicited — the SME confirmed both patterns but did not diagnose root causes.

Description.evidence: the authorised approver pool is small, so when the named approver is on leave or at lunch near cut-off, items bottleneck waiting for a second pair of eyes

Impact.evidence: items bottleneck waiting for a second pair of eyes — most acute near the 14:00 CET cut-off where delay risks same-day-value failure

content.title: Small Authorised Approver Pool Causing Bottleneck at Second-Line Approval
content.description: The pool of individuals authorised to act as Operations Approver for second-line approval is small. When the available approver is on leave or unavailable near the cut-off, items queue waiting for a second pair of eyes.
content.impact: Items bottleneck at the 4-eyes step, most acutely near the 14:00 CET same-day-value cut-off where delays directly risk missed same-day settlement.
content.rootCause: Not elicited — the SME confirmed the pool is small but did not diagnose whether the constraint is policy-driven, a capacity decision, or a licensing/access-rights issue.

Description.evidence: occasional CBS latency at peak, and rare failed postings that need manual investigation

Impact.evidence: failed postings that need manual investigation — delays execution and adds analyst handling time

content.title: CBS Latency and Failed Postings at Execute Release
content.description: At peak volume the Core Banking System occasionally runs slowly, and a small number of postings fail with technical errors, duplicates or settlement rejects that require manual analyst investigation and a controlled retry.
content.impact: Failed postings halt the release at the execution step and consume analyst time on investigation and retry; combined with peak-period CBS latency, this can push items past the same-day-value cut-off.
content.rootCause: Not elicited — the SME described CBS latency and rare posting failures at peak but did not diagnose the underlying CBS architecture or capacity cause.

Description.evidence: end-of-day closure backlog when many items complete together near cut-off, and occasional notification failures

Impact.evidence: end-of-day closure backlog near cut-off; occasional notification failures — no automatic retry today, analyst follows up manually

content.title: End-of-Day Closure Backlog and Notification Failures at Confirm and Close
content.description: Two friction points at the final step: (1) when many items complete near the cut-off simultaneously, the closure queue creates an end-of-day backlog; (2) the automated Front Office notification occasionally fails, and there is no automatic retry — the analyst must follow up manually.
content.impact: Closure backlog slows the formal completion of items near cut-off, creating a reporting and reconciliation lag. Notification failures leave the Front Office unconfirmed until the analyst follows up, creating a client-experience gap and a manual handling overhead.
content.rootCause: Not elicited — the SME confirmed both patterns but did not diagnose whether the notification failure root cause is tool reliability, network, or configuration.

content.title: No Formal Intake Completeness Control at Receive Request
content.theGap: The Receive Request step (PS-FRDB-001) has no formally documented completeness control. The Payments Workflow Tool enforces mandatory field entry, but this is not defined, monitored or reported as a named control.
content.risk: Items may enter the queue with incomplete or incorrectly keyed data — particularly those manually entered from email or phone submissions — creating traceability gaps and downstream validation failures.
content.remediation: Formal intake completeness control drafted as CP-FRDB-007 (Intake Completeness Control), covering both automated PWT mandatory-field enforcement and manual email/phone intake checklists. Gap is now addressed.

content.title: Real-time FO status visibility and cut-off-aware queue priority
content.targetDescription: The Payments Workflow Tool emits a status event to the Front Office at every major approval-stage transition. Items approaching the 14:00 CET cut-off are automatically flagged in the queue, enabling the operations team to prioritise resolution. The second-line approval step is maintained as a regulatory control, but approvers receive AI-enriched context from the pre-validation agent and real-time cut-off proximity indicators.
whatChanges: PWT emits a stage-transition event to Front Office when an item reaches first-line approval, second-line approval, Treasury confirmation and execution — replacing the current black-box queue
Queue items are auto-flagged as cut-off-risk when fewer than 90 minutes remain before the 14:00 CET cut-off, triggering a priority lane in the operations queue
Second-line approval (four-eyes control) is retained per EBA/GL/2021/05; the approver now receives the AI pre-validation summary alongside the usual approval request
Front Office status notifications replace reactive phone escalation, reducing inbound call volume to Operations
content.rationale: Removing the black-box queue eliminates the reactive escalation loop and supports the cut-off; maintaining four-eyes control satisfies EBA/GL/2021/05 while AI context improves approver decision quality.

content.title: Upstream beneficiary-account verification with confirmation-of-payee
content.targetDescription: At portal submission, the drawdown request is checked against a confirmation-of-payee service to verify that the beneficiary account name matches the registered holder. Mismatches and unverified accounts are surfaced to the Front Office at intake — before the item enters the queue. The execution callback path, currently used to resolve beneficiary discrepancies after approval, is eliminated for all CoP-verified items and becomes a documented exception only.
whatChanges: Live CoP/beneficiary-account verification is performed at portal submission, before the item enters the Operations queue
Beneficiary mismatches or unverifiable accounts are returned to Front Office at intake with a structured explanation, eliminating the post-approval callback path
The execution callback step (PS-FRDB-007) is retained only as an exception path for non-CoP-eligible payment types; it is no longer the default resolution mechanism for beneficiary issues
Beneficiary verification results are recorded on the drawdown case record, creating an audit trail at the earliest point in the process
content.rationale: Moving beneficiary verification upstream eliminates the most disruptive late-stage callback — a confirmed waste from both the FP-FRDB-003 friction point and the regulatory need for robust payment data at entry.

content.title: Deploy structured digital drawdown portal with live FMS limit feed
content.theDecision: Deploy a structured digital drawdown portal for front-office and corporate client submission, backed by a real-time FMS API integration that provides live facility status and limit at the point of intake.
optionsConsidered: Build a bespoke portal on top of the Payments Workflow Tool (build)
License a commercial corporate lending portal such as nCino or Finastra (buy)
Extend the existing PWT intake form with mandatory-field enforcement only, without FMS real-time feed (minimal option)
content.rationale: A structured portal is the only option that enforces completeness at the point of submission and removes the FMS staleness risk simultaneously. The minimal option removes email/phone intake but leaves the FMS stale-data gap open. Build vs buy is deferred to the IT-architecture pass.

content.title: Augment Sanctions Screening Engine with AI/ML false-positive reduction layer
content.theDecision: Deploy an AI/ML layer on top of the existing Sanctions Screening Engine that applies contextual entity resolution and behavioral scoring to auto-dismiss low-probability alerts before they reach the Compliance adjudication queue.
optionsConsidered: Replace the rules-based engine entirely with an AI-native screening platform such as ComplyAdvantage Mesh or Napier AI (rip-and-replace)
Add an AI secondary-scoring layer on top of the existing engine, routing only high-confidence hits to Compliance (augment)
Expand Compliance headcount to absorb the backlog without changing the engine (do nothing to the technology)
content.rationale: Augmenting the existing engine is lower risk and faster than rip-and-replace: the rules-based engine remains as the primary gate while the AI layer acts as a filter. Headcount expansion is not a sustainable response to a structurally high false-positive rate. EBA AI guidelines make augmentation a regulatory expectation.

content.title: Deploy AI pre-validation agent for approval package with mandatory acknowledgement
content.theDecision: Deploy an AI agent that pre-assembles the approval package — facility-check results, screening outcome and value date — and presents a structured summary the Operations Analyst must explicitly acknowledge before the first-line approval action is enabled.
optionsConsidered: Redesign the PWT approval screen to surface detail prominently without an AI agent (UI-only change)
Deploy an AI agent that pre-assembles the package and forces acknowledgement before approval (AI pre-validation)
Automate first-line approval entirely for items below a risk threshold (full automation)
content.rationale: UI-only redesign may not be sufficient under queue pressure — a mandatory acknowledgement step changes the workflow, not just the display. Full automation is deferred: the 4-eyes control must remain with a human for now, and EU AI Act implications require careful scoping before a credit-adjacent automated approval is deployed.

content.title: Automate Treasury liquidity confirmation via PWT-to-Treasury Platform API
content.theDecision: Integrate the Payments Workflow Tool with the Treasury / Liquidity Platform via API to automate the funding-availability check for in-scope items with clear headroom, subject to ALCO-approved thresholds.
optionsConsidered: Expand the Treasury team to reduce confirmation wait time near cut-off (headcount approach)
Automate routine confirmations via PWT-to-Treasury Platform API within ALCO-approved thresholds (API automation)
Extend the same-day-value cut-off to 16:00 CET to give more time for manual confirmation (cut-off extension)
content.rationale: API automation removes the human-availability constraint for routine items, which is the structural cause of cut-off risk. Headcount expansion addresses symptoms, not the root cause. Cut-off extension requires payment infrastructure changes and may conflict with SEPA Instant obligations. ALCO governance on thresholds is the necessary constraint on the automation.

content.title: Replace CBS retry and FO notification with event-driven idempotent infrastructure
content.theDecision: Replace the current single-attempt CBS posting retry and the single-attempt FO notification with event-driven idempotent infrastructure: automatic controlled retry with escalation after N failed attempts, and a guaranteed-delivery outbound notification queue.
optionsConsidered: Retain manual analyst investigation and retry; invest in CBS capacity to reduce peak failure rates (do nothing to the workflow)
Implement event-driven retry and guaranteed-delivery notification queue at PWT layer (event-driven architecture)
Replace the Core Banking System to eliminate the posting failure pattern at source (CBS replacement)
content.rationale: Manual retry has no control value and consumes analyst time; the SEPA Instant notification mandate makes guaranteed delivery a regulatory requirement, not a convenience. CBS replacement is disproportionate to the failure rate — a retry layer at the PWT is the proportionate intervention. Idempotency is the safety constraint that prevents duplicate postings.

content.title: Integrate confirmation-of-payee beneficiary-account check at portal submission
content.theDecision: Integrate a confirmation-of-payee or beneficiary-account verification service into the digital drawdown portal at the submission step, so that every release request is beneficiary-verified before it enters the Operations queue.
optionsConsidered: License a third-party CoP/account-verification API from a provider within the bank's operating jurisdiction (buy)
Build an in-house beneficiary registry based on past-release records and CBS account data, without external CoP service (internal build)
Retain the current post-approval callback as the beneficiary resolution mechanism, with no upstream change (do nothing)
content.rationale: An external CoP service is the fastest path to coverage and aligns with emerging EU payment data-quality requirements under IPR 2024/886. Internal build risks coverage gaps for new counterparties. Doing nothing preserves the callback inefficiency confirmed as a primary friction point by the payment-operations SME.

content.title: Implement PWT status-event push and cut-off-aware queue priority flag
content.theDecision: Build a status-event push from the Payments Workflow Tool to Front Office, and add a time-to-cut-off priority flag in the operations queue so items at risk of missing the 14:00 CET deadline are surfaced before they stall.
optionsConsidered: Implement push notifications from PWT to Front Office with cut-off-proximity flag in the operations queue (targeted build)
Provide a read-only FO status dashboard with polling refresh, without proactive push notifications (pull model)
Retain the current manual-call escalation model for FO status enquiries (do nothing)
content.rationale: Push over pull eliminates the reactive call loop: FO does not need to chase status because status comes to them. The cut-off flag addresses the near-cut-off Treasury friction confirmed by the SME and directly reduces same-day value miss risk without requiring a cut-off time extension.

content.title: Front Office channel migration to digital portal
content.theGap: A significant proportion of front-office drawdown requests currently arrive by email or phone. Until Front Office staff and clients migrate to the digital portal, the benefits of TD-1 are not realised and manual keying overhead persists.
content.resolution: A structured change-management programme is required: communication, training and adoption incentives for Front Office staff; client outreach for corporate borrowers. Manual keying is maintained as a documented exception path with a time-limited sunset. SLA reporting should distinguish portal-submitted from off-channel items to create adoption pressure.

content.title: FMS API readiness for real-time limit feed
content.theGap: The Facility Management System may not expose a real-time API or event stream. If the FMS is batch-only, the live limit feed required by TD-1 needs a polling adapter or a vendor upgrade before the digital portal can display real-time limit data.
content.resolution: Conduct a technical assessment of the FMS vendor's API capabilities. If a real-time or near-real-time API is available, integrate directly. If not, deploy a thin polling-with-cache adapter at the PWT layer, with a cache TTL short enough to make the staleness window acceptable.
